title: The Science Fiction Small Box Game Shoot Out
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ac6AOC9yxJE

Daniel (@dungeondive) stages a small-box shootout between four science-fiction games — Dark Venture, Hunted: Mining Colony 415, Space Hulk: Death Angel (the card game), and Deep Space D-6 (paired with its The Long Way Home solo-RPG supplement) — ranking them across four categories.

For art, Dark Venture (Robert Lemon's superb work) takes it — and he notes a reprint is coming, with his tweaked solo rules now officially included. For "big-game feel," Dark Venture again wins, cramming an almost overstuffed epic into a small box. For best use of the science-fiction *idea* (his favourite thing about SF in books — he riffs on Dick, Sturgeon, Bester), Deep Space D-6 with The Long Way Home wins, genuinely evoking a space-opera of crew assignment and hard captain's choices. And for which he most wants to play, Deep Space D-6 surprises him into first — a game he nearly sold now beloved — while Dark Venture lands third for being too long and table-hungry, and Death Angel last for punishing difficulty.

A thoughtful comparative piece that doubles as a meditation on why board-game SF so often delivers setting and aesthetic but rarely *ideas*.

----

Which board game best delivers science-fiction *ideas* (not just the aesthetic) for you — Daniel's looking for recommendations?
